meta.title: Swire RGM Certification Program
meta.assessmentFocus: Annual certification model with official pillar scoring and diagnostic stage and enabler views.

scoreModel.gatingRule: If any pillar score is below 10 / 20, the highest possible overall certification is Established.

pillars.label: Pricing
pillars.description: Manage pricing direction, pricing architecture, price execution, and pricing governance to support sustainable NSR / GP / value creation.

pillars.label: OBPPC
pillars.description: Manage occasion-brand-pack-price-channel architecture and pack-price role design to match shopper, occasion, and channel opportunity.

pillars.label: Mix
pillars.description: Manage portfolio, channel, customer, and product mix direction to improve revenue quality, NSR, GP, and long-term profit pool quality.

pillars.label: Promotion Spend
pillars.description: Manage promotion strategy, planning, execution, analysis, and learning so spend supports clear commercial objectives and value creation.

pillars.label: DFR / Trade Investment
pillars.description: Manage DFR, trade terms, rebates, commercial policy, pay-for-performance, and customer value exchange to ensure trade investment is strategic and measurable.

stages.label: Identify the Opportunity
stages.description: Identify the most important opportunity areas, pain points, and value pools.

stages.label: Shape the Strategy
stages.description: Translate insights into clear strategic choices and direction.

stages.label: Set Commercial Policy
stages.description: Turn strategy into rules, guardrails, KPIs, targets, and management requirements.

stages.label: Win In-Market
stages.description: Drive disciplined execution, monitoring, and corrective action in market.

stages.label: Build for the Future
stages.description: Institutionalize capability, strengthen teams and governance, and improve future readiness.

enablers.label: Governance
enablers.description: Decision rights, routines, reviews, accountability, and cross-functional ways of working.

enablers.label: Digital / Data / Tools
enablers.description: Data quality, tools, dashboards, reporting, analytics, and decision support.

enablers.label: People & Capability Development
enablers.description: Role clarity, capability building, coaching, and cross-functional understanding.

enablers.label: Forward-looking
enablers.description: Long-term view, future opportunity mapping, proactive improvement, and continuous refresh.

questions.text: Pricing opportunity and issue diagnosis

anchors.1: The capability is at an early stage. Pricing discussions are mainly reactive, with limited clarity on where the most important opportunities and issues sit.
anchors.2: Some pricing opportunities and issues are recognized, but the view is still fragmented across categories, channels, or customers.
anchors.3: The main pricing opportunities and issues are identified across key business areas and are used in regular discussion.
anchors.4: Pricing opportunities and risks are assessed in a structured way across categories, channels, and customers to support prioritization.
anchors.5: A forward-looking pricing opportunity and risk view is maintained and proactively used to guide strategic focus and resource allocation.

subItems.text: The main pricing opportunities and pain points have been identified.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use OBPPC for pack-price role or whitespace questions.

subItems.text: Pricing issues are differentiated by category, channel, or customer.
A clear pricing opportunity / risk map is in place rather than isolated observations.

questions.text: Use of pricing-related data and insight inputs

anchors.1: Pricing decisions rely mainly on broad observations, with limited use of structured data or external insight.
anchors.2: Some pricing-related data and customer input are used, but the coverage and consistency remain limited.
anchors.3: Relevant market, competitor, and customer inputs are used in key pricing discussions, though depth varies by context.
anchors.4: Multiple pricing inputs, including market, competitor, shopper, and customer data, are used consistently to support diagnosis.
anchors.5: A strong, integrated pricing insight base is used proactively to identify opportunities, pressure points, and future moves.

subItems.text: Market and competitor pricing data are used in pricing diagnosis.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Trade Investment for contract / rebate structure questions.

subItems.text: Shopper, elasticity, or price perception inputs are used in pricing assessment.
Customer feedback or frontline input is used to support pricing decisions.

questions.text: Long-term pricing strategy and direction

anchors.1: Pricing direction is still evolving and is not yet clearly defined over the medium to long term.
anchors.2: An initial pricing direction exists, but it is not yet fully translated across categories, channels, or customer segments.
anchors.3: A clear pricing direction is defined for the main business areas and supports regular decision-making.
anchors.4: Long-term pricing direction is clearly defined across major categories and channels and aligned with growth and profitability goals.
anchors.5: A forward-looking pricing strategy is fully articulated, dynamically maintained, and used to shape long-term value creation.

subItems.text: A clear long-term pricing strategy direction has been defined.
Price direction is defined across major categories, channels, or segments.
Pricing direction is aligned with business growth and profitability ambitions.

questions.text: Translation of insights into pricing strategy choices

anchors.1: Insights are discussed, but they are not yet translated into clear pricing choices or principles.
anchors.2: Some pricing choices are informed by insights, though translation remains inconsistent and dependent on individual judgment.
anchors.3: Insights are regularly translated into pricing choices in the main business context, with basic cross-functional alignment.
anchors.4: Insights are systematically translated into clear pricing choices, with strong cross-functional discussion and alignment.
anchors.5: Insight-to-action translation is highly disciplined and consistently turns pricing analysis into practical strategic choices.

subItems.text: Pricing insights are translated into clear strategic choices.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Promotion Spend for promo intensity / promo guideline questions.

subItems.text: Cross-functional discussion is used to align pricing choices.
Pricing strategy is translated into practical decision principles.

questions.text: Pricing guardrails and commercial rules

anchors.1: Pricing guardrails are still forming and are not yet consistently defined or applied.
anchors.2: Some pricing rules exist, but they are not yet clear, consistent, or widely understood across teams.
anchors.3: Basic pricing guardrails are defined and used across the main commercial context, though some gaps remain.
anchors.4: Clear pricing guardrails and approval rules are in place and applied consistently across channels and customers.
anchors.5: Pricing guardrails are robust, well understood, and actively used to guide disciplined commercial decisions at scale.

subItems.text: Clear pricing principles or guardrails are in place.
Pricing rules are differentiated across channels or customers where relevant.
Pricing approval or escalation logic is clearly defined.

questions.text: Pricing KPI and target setting

anchors.1: Pricing KPIs and targets are not yet clearly defined or regularly used.
anchors.2: Some pricing metrics are used, but targets and tolerance levels are still limited or inconsistent.
anchors.3: Pricing KPIs and targets are defined for the main business areas and support regular monitoring.
anchors.4: Pricing KPIs, targets, and tolerance bands are clearly defined and supported by structured reporting.
anchors.5: Pricing KPI management is strong, forward-looking, and tightly linked to commercial action and performance control.

subItems.text: Pricing-related KPIs have been defined.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Mix for revenue-quality / mix KPI questions.

subItems.text: Pricing targets, thresholds, or tolerance bands are defined.
Pricing KPIs are supported by regular reporting or dashboards.

questions.text: Price execution and compliance management

anchors.1: Price execution is reviewed in selected cases, but compliance management is not yet consistent.
anchors.2: Some price deviations are identified and addressed, though management remains uneven and mostly reactive.
anchors.3: Price execution and compliance are tracked in the main business context, with basic corrective action in place.
anchors.4: Price compliance is managed systematically, and deviations are identified and resolved through clear routines.
anchors.5: Price execution is tightly managed, with rapid issue resolution, strong discipline, and closed-loop corrective action.

subItems.text: Actual price execution and compliance are tracked.
Price deviations or non-compliance can be identified.
Corrective action is taken to close price execution gaps.

questions.text: Price review routine and response mechanism

anchors.1: Pricing reviews happen irregularly and are not yet a consistent basis for action.
anchors.2: Regular pricing reviews are starting to form, but response speed and decision quality remain uneven.
anchors.3: Pricing reviews take place on a regular basis and support timely action on key issues.
anchors.4: Pricing reviews are structured, insight-led, and enable consistent responses to competitor and market changes.
anchors.5: Pricing reviews are highly effective, data-supported, and drive rapid, disciplined, and forward-looking responses.

subItems.text: A regular pricing review cadence is in place.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Promotion Spend for promotion review questions.

subItems.text: Pricing reviews respond to competitor or market changes.
Pricing reviews support timely decision-making.

questions.text: Pricing capability building

anchors.1: Pricing knowledge is concentrated in a small number of individuals and is not yet broadly built across teams.
anchors.2: Some pricing capability building exists, but it is still limited in reach or consistency.
anchors.3: Basic pricing capability is being built across relevant functions and supports the main pricing routines.
anchors.4: Pricing capability is developed in a structured way across commercial, sales, and finance teams.
anchors.5: Pricing capability building is embedded, role-based, and continuously strengthened across the organization.

subItems.text: Pricing capability building or coaching is in place.
Sales, finance, and commercial teams share a common understanding of pricing.
Pricing capability does not rely only on a few experts.

questions.text: Continuous improvement and future pricing readiness

anchors.1: Pricing decisions are reviewed only occasionally, and future readiness is still limited.
anchors.2: Some learning from pricing actions is captured, but continuous improvement is not yet systematic.
anchors.3: Pricing learnings are reviewed and used to refine future decisions in the main business context.
anchors.4: A clear learning and improvement loop exists, with pricing strategy regularly updated based on results and market shifts.
anchors.5: Pricing management is continuously improved, highly adaptive, and proactively prepared for future opportunity and risk.

subItems.text: Pricing actions are reviewed and lessons are captured.
Pricing strategy is refreshed over time rather than left unchanged.
Pricing management considers future market change, risk, and opportunity.

questions.text: Occasion / shopper / channel opportunity identification

anchors.1: OBPPC discussions are still developing and are based on a limited view of occasion, shopper, and channel opportunity.
anchors.2: Some occasion, shopper, or channel opportunities are recognized, but the view is still partial and not yet integrated.
anchors.3: The main occasion, shopper, and channel opportunities are identified and used in core portfolio discussions.
anchors.4: Opportunity identification is structured and informs clear OBPPC prioritization across channels and missions.
anchors.5: A forward-looking opportunity map is maintained and actively shapes OBPPC choices and portfolio evolution.

subItems.text: Key occasion and shopper mission opportunities have been identified.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Mix when the question is primarily about customer / channel / revenue-quality steering.

subItems.text: The main portfolio opportunities by channel have been identified.
Important whitespace or unmet needs have been identified.

questions.text: Use of market, shopper, and competitor inputs for OBPPC

anchors.1: OBPPC choices rely mainly on internal experience, with limited use of shopper, market, or competitor input.
anchors.2: Some relevant inputs are used, but coverage and consistency remain limited across business areas.
anchors.3: Shopper, market, and competitor inputs are used in the main OBPPC discussions and support basic decision quality.
anchors.4: A broad and relevant input base is used consistently to support OBPPC diagnosis and architecture choices.
anchors.5: Integrated and high-quality input sources are used proactively to shape forward-looking OBPPC decisions.

subItems.text: Shopper, customer, or market insight is used in OBPPC analysis.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Mix for profitability-by-customer or revenue mix analysis.

subItems.text: Competitor pack-price architecture or whitespace is analyzed.
Local data is used rather than relying only on experience.

questions.text: OBPPC strategic direction and architecture

anchors.1: OBPPC direction is still evolving and has not yet been translated into a clear architecture.
anchors.2: A broad OBPPC direction exists, but the architecture is not yet fully defined or consistently used.
anchors.3: A clear OBPPC direction is in place and supports the main portfolio and channel decisions.
anchors.4: OBPPC architecture is well defined, actionable, and aligned with market opportunity and strategic priorities.
anchors.5: OBPPC architecture is forward-looking, consistently applied, and actively shapes portfolio development over time.

subItems.text: A clear OBPPC strategic direction has been defined.
Occasion or channel opportunity has been translated into portfolio architecture.
OBPPC direction is aligned to market reality and growth priorities.

questions.text: Pack role and channel role definition

anchors.1: Pack and channel roles are still forming and are not yet clearly defined or aligned.
anchors.2: Some roles are defined, but coverage and understanding remain uneven across teams or channels.
anchors.3: The main pack and channel roles are defined and used in regular planning and commercial discussion.
anchors.4: Pack and channel roles are clearly defined, differentiated, and consistently understood across functions.
anchors.5: Role definitions are highly clear, embedded, and used proactively to guide architecture and in-market decisions.

subItems.text: The roles of the main packs have been clearly defined.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Mix for mix ambition or what should be pushed by channel / customer.

subItems.text: Pack roles are differentiated by channel or occasion where relevant.
Functions share a common understanding of pack roles.

questions.text: Translation of OBPPC strategy into portfolio rules

anchors.1: OBPPC strategy is not yet translated into clear portfolio rules or deployment principles.
anchors.2: Some portfolio principles exist, but they are not yet complete or consistently applied.
anchors.3: Basic portfolio rules are in place and guide the main deployment and rationalization choices.
anchors.4: OBPPC strategy is translated into clear and consistent portfolio rules that support disciplined decision-making.
anchors.5: A robust OBPPC rule set is embedded and actively used to shape deployment, innovation, and portfolio evolution.

subItems.text: OBPPC strategy has been translated into portfolio or deployment rules.
Basic innovation or rationalization principles are in place.
These rules can be applied consistently across teams.

questions.text: OBPPC KPI and tracking framework

anchors.1: OBPPC KPI definition and tracking are still limited and not yet consistent.
anchors.2: Some OBPPC indicators are used, but coverage and reporting remain incomplete.
anchors.3: Basic OBPPC KPIs are tracked and support regular visibility on key portfolio and channel outcomes.
anchors.4: OBPPC KPIs and reporting are clearly defined and support consistent performance management.
anchors.5: OBPPC tracking is strong, decision-oriented, and supports continuous architecture refinement.

subItems.text: OBPPC KPIs have been defined.
KPIs can be tracked by channel, pack, or portfolio.
Regular reporting or dashboards support OBPPC tracking.

questions.text: Execution of OBPPC in market

anchors.1: OBPPC execution is uneven and not yet consistently linked to the intended architecture.
anchors.2: Some OBPPC principles are executed in market, but deviations are common and not always managed.
anchors.3: The main OBPPC priorities are implemented in market and are reviewed in the core business context.
anchors.4: OBPPC execution is managed systematically, with clear visibility on gaps and corrective action.
anchors.5: OBPPC execution is highly disciplined, consistently aligned to strategy, and continuously improved in market.

subItems.text: Actual OBPPC execution in market or at customer level is tracked.
Deviations between intended architecture and market execution can be identified.
Corrective action is taken when execution deviates from the intended architecture.

questions.text: OBPPC performance monitoring and learning

anchors.1: OBPPC results are reviewed only selectively, with limited structured learning.
anchors.2: Some review and learning take place, but they are still ad hoc and uneven across channels or packs.
anchors.3: OBPPC performance is monitored in the main business context, and lessons are used in future discussion.
anchors.4: A clear monitoring and learning loop exists and is used to refine OBPPC choices over time.
anchors.5: OBPPC performance learning is highly disciplined and directly strengthens future architecture and market decisions.

subItems.text: Key pack or portfolio performance is reviewed regularly.
The organization extracts actionable learning from channel results.
Those learnings are fed back into future OBPPC decisions.

questions.text: OBPPC capability and cross-functional ownership

anchors.1: OBPPC capability is still concentrated in a small group and is not yet broadly owned across functions.
anchors.2: Cross-functional involvement is increasing, but understanding and ownership remain uneven.
anchors.3: The main functions share a basic OBPPC understanding and contribute to regular decision-making.
anchors.4: OBPPC capability and ownership are well developed across relevant functions and support consistent execution.
anchors.5: OBPPC capability is broadly embedded, well owned cross-functionally, and continuously strengthened.

subItems.text: OBPPC capability building or coaching is in place.
Marketing, sales, finance, and supply chain collaborate on OBPPC.
OBPPC does not rely only on a small number of individuals.

questions.text: OBPPC future-readiness and innovation linkage

anchors.1: OBPPC is used mainly for current decisions, with limited linkage to future whitespace or innovation.
anchors.2: Some future opportunities are considered, but linkage to OBPPC design is still emerging.
anchors.3: OBPPC is linked to selected future opportunities and supports parts of the innovation discussion.
anchors.4: A structured link exists between OBPPC, innovation, and future opportunity areas.
anchors.5: OBPPC is fully connected to future whitespace, innovation planning, and long-term portfolio evolution.

subItems.text: OBPPC is linked to innovation or future whitespace discussion.
OBPPC architecture is refreshed over time as the market changes.
OBPPC is used with a future-oriented rather than purely reactive mindset.

questions.text: Mix opportunity and value pool identification

anchors.1: Mix management is still at an early stage, with limited clarity on where the most important opportunities sit.
anchors.2: Some mix opportunities are recognized, but the view remains partial across category, channel, or customer dimensions.
anchors.3: The main mix gaps and value pools are identified and used in regular commercial discussion.
anchors.4: Mix opportunities are diagnosed in a structured way across category, channel, customer, and revenue quality dimensions.
anchors.5: A forward-looking mix opportunity map is actively maintained and used to guide strategic prioritization.

subItems.text: The main mix improvement opportunities have been identified.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use OBPPC if the question is primarily about pack role, price-pack ladder, or architecture design.

subItems.text: Key category, channel, or customer mix gaps have been identified.
The main value pools for improving NSR / GP / revenue quality through mix are understood.

questions.text: Use of profitability and mix drivers in diagnosis

anchors.1: Mix diagnosis relies mainly on high-level outcomes, with limited use of profitability or mix driver analysis.
anchors.2: Some profitability and mix driver analysis is used, but not yet consistently or at sufficient depth.
anchors.3: Basic profitability and mix driver analysis supports mix diagnosis in the main business context.
anchors.4: Mix diagnosis is supported by strong use of profitability, commercial, and mix driver data across key dimensions.
anchors.5: A robust, multi-source diagnostic base is used proactively to identify and prioritize high-value mix opportunities.

subItems.text: Profitability by SKU, channel, or customer is used in mix diagnosis.
Mix drivers are analyzed rather than only reviewing total outcomes.
Relevant sell-in, sell-out, scan, or commercial data is available to support mix diagnosis.

questions.text: Mix ambition and strategic direction

anchors.1: Mix ambition is still forming and is not yet clearly defined across the business.
anchors.2: An initial mix direction exists, but it is not yet explicit across category, channel, or customer dimensions.
anchors.3: A basic mix ambition is defined and linked to the main revenue and profitability priorities.
anchors.4: Clear mix ambition and direction are defined across key business dimensions and used in strategy discussions.
anchors.5: A strong, forward-looking mix strategy is in place and actively guides commercial focus and resource allocation.

subItems.text: A clear mix ambition has been defined.
Mix direction has been defined by category, channel, or customer where relevant.
Mix direction is aligned to NSR, GP, or revenue-quality objectives.

questions.text: Translation of mix strategy into commercial priorities

anchors.1: Mix ambition is not yet translated into clear commercial priorities or execution direction.
anchors.2: Some mix priorities are identified, but they are not yet fully actionable or consistently applied.
anchors.3: Mix ambition is translated into basic commercial priorities in the main business context.
anchors.4: Mix strategy is clearly translated into channel, customer, and assortment priorities that support action.
anchors.5: Mix priorities are sharply defined, broadly aligned, and consistently used to steer execution and decision-making.

subItems.text: Mix strategy has been translated into clear commercial priorities.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use OBPPC for pack role, shelf pack role, or price-pack ladder questions.

subItems.text: Mix ambition has been translated into picture of success or assortment direction.
Mix priorities are aligned to brand, channel, or customer strategy.

questions.text: Mix KPI, targets, and steering rules

anchors.1: Mix KPIs and steering rules are still limited and not yet clearly defined.
anchors.2: Some mix indicators and targets exist, but they are not yet comprehensive or consistently used.
anchors.3: Basic mix KPIs and targets are in place and support regular performance discussion.
anchors.4: Clear mix KPIs, targets, and steering rules are defined and used across key business dimensions.
anchors.5: Mix management is supported by a strong KPI framework that drives disciplined and value-oriented steering.

subItems.text: Mix-related KPIs have been defined.
Mix targets exist by channel, customer, or category where relevant.
Mix steering rules or management guardrails are in place.

questions.text: Use of tools and reporting to support mix steering

anchors.1: Mix reporting is limited and does not yet support regular steering.
anchors.2: Basic reporting exists, but timeliness, depth, or usability remain inconsistent.
anchors.3: Regular mix reporting supports core visibility and basic decision-making.
anchors.4: Mix reporting and tools provide clear, consistent support for multi-dimensional review and steering.
anchors.5: Mix steering is enabled by strong, decision-oriented tools and reporting that support continuous optimization.

subItems.text: Regular mix reporting is in place.
Mix performance can be tracked clearly and regularly.
Tools or reporting support mix decision-making.

questions.text: Execution of mix priorities in market

anchors.1: Mix priorities are not yet consistently translated into market or customer execution.
anchors.2: Some mix priorities are reflected in execution, but application is uneven and not yet well tracked.
anchors.3: The main mix priorities are translated into market actions and reviewed in the core business context.
anchors.4: Mix priorities are consistently embedded in sales and customer execution, with visible tracking and follow-up.
anchors.5: Mix execution is disciplined, scalable, and consistently aligned to strategic revenue-quality objectives.

subItems.text: Mix priorities have been translated into sales or customer execution requirements.
Assortment, picture of success, or mix priorities are tracked in execution.
Corrective action is taken when mix execution gaps are identified.

questions.text: Mix performance review and actioning

anchors.1: Mix results are reviewed only selectively, with limited action based on the findings.
anchors.2: Some mix review takes place, but actions are still uneven or mostly reactive.
anchors.3: Mix performance is reviewed regularly, and the main findings inform corrective actions.
anchors.4: Mix review is structured across key dimensions and consistently translated into commercial actions.
anchors.5: A strong mix learning and action loop is in place and continually improves performance and decision quality.

subItems.text: Mix results are reviewed regularly.
Results can be reviewed at channel, customer, or category level.
Review outcomes are translated into concrete actions.

questions.text: Mix capability and organizational understanding

anchors.1: Mix understanding is still limited and not yet broadly embedded beyond a small number of individuals.
anchors.2: Some teams understand mix concepts, but capability and ownership remain uneven across functions.
anchors.3: A basic shared understanding of mix exists across the main functions, with early capability building in place.
anchors.4: Mix capability is built in a structured way across functions and supports stronger commercial decisions.
anchors.5: Mix is widely understood as a management discipline and supported by strong, continuous capability development.

subItems.text: Mix capability building is in place.
Sales, finance, and commercial teams share a common understanding of mix.
Mix management is not limited to financial reporting only.

questions.text: Expansion and future scaling of mix steering

anchors.1: Mix steering is used in selected areas only and is not yet planned for broader scaling.
anchors.2: There is intent to scale mix steering, but application remains limited and uneven.
anchors.3: Mix steering is expanding across more channels, customers, or categories, with a basic roadmap in place.
anchors.4: Mix steering is being scaled systematically and continuously refined across the organization.
anchors.5: Mix steering is fully embedded as a scalable, forward-looking capability that supports long-term value creation.

subItems.text: Mix steering is being extended across more channels, customers, or categories.
Mix logic is continuously improved rather than treated as a one-off analysis.
Mix steering is reflected in longer-term planning or capability building.

questions.text: Promotion opportunity and issue diagnosis

anchors.1: Promotion discussions are still mainly reactive, with limited clarity on where the biggest opportunities and issues sit.
anchors.2: Some promotion issues and opportunities are identified, but the view is still fragmented across customers, packs, or channels.
anchors.3: The main promotion opportunities and issues are identified in the core business context and inform planning.
anchors.4: Promotion opportunities and pressure points are diagnosed in a structured way across key dimensions.
anchors.5: A forward-looking promotion opportunity view is actively used to shape priorities, investment, and strategic choices.

subItems.text: The main promotion opportunities and pain points have been identified.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use DFR / Trade Investment for rebate, funding structure, or trade term design questions.

subItems.text: Promotion issues are differentiated by channel, customer, or pack where relevant.
A clear promotion opportunity / risk view is in place rather than only historical review.

questions.text: Use of promotion data and feedback inputs

anchors.1: Promotion diagnosis relies mostly on broad experience, with limited use of structured data or feedback.
anchors.2: Some promotion data and market feedback are used, but depth and consistency remain limited.
anchors.3: Relevant promotion data and selected feedback inputs support regular diagnosis in the main business context.
anchors.4: Promotion diagnosis is consistently supported by structured data, feedback, and performance signals.
anchors.5: A strong promotion insight base is used proactively to identify future opportunity, inefficiency, and investment choices.

subItems.text: Historical promotion data is used in promotion diagnosis.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use OBPPC for shopper mission and pack-price design questions.

subItems.text: Customer or market feedback is used to assess promotion choices.
Baseline, uplift, or efficiency-related signals are used in promotion diagnosis.

questions.text: Promotion strategy and objective setting

anchors.1: Promotion direction is still forming, and objectives are not yet consistently defined.
anchors.2: Some promotion objectives are set, but they are still broad or not clearly differentiated.
anchors.3: Promotion strategy and objectives are defined for the main business priorities and support planning.
anchors.4: Promotion strategy is clearly linked to differentiated objectives such as recruit, defend, trade up, or accelerate.
anchors.5: Promotion strategy is highly intentional, future-oriented, and clearly aligned to category growth and value creation.

subItems.text: A clear promotion strategy has been defined.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Pricing for long-term pricing direction questions.

subItems.text: Promotion objectives are differentiated (for example recruit, defend, trade up, accelerate).
Promotion strategy is aligned to category growth or value creation logic.

questions.text: Translation into promotion guidelines and plans

anchors.1: Promotion strategy is not yet consistently translated into practical guidelines and plans.
anchors.2: Some guidelines and plans exist, but they are not yet fully differentiated or consistently applied.
anchors.3: Promotion strategy is translated into basic guidelines and plans in the main commercial context.
anchors.4: Clear promotion guidelines and differentiated plans are in place across key channels, packs, or customers.
anchors.5: Promotion strategy is translated into highly actionable, differentiated plans that are widely understood and consistently applied.

subItems.text: Promotion strategy has been translated into clear guidelines.
Promotion plans are differentiated by channel, customer, or pack where relevant.
Promotion plans include a clear planning logic or expected impact view.

questions.text: Promotion approval rules and management guardrails

anchors.1: Promotion approval and guardrails are still developing and are not yet used consistently.
anchors.2: Some approval rules and limits exist, but coverage and consistency remain uneven.
anchors.3: Basic promotion approval rules and guardrails are in place and support regular management.
anchors.4: Promotion approval, budget, intensity, and frequency guardrails are clearly defined and consistently applied.
anchors.5: Promotion governance is strong, disciplined, and actively supports high-quality, value-oriented decision-making.

subItems.text: Promotion approval rules are in place.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use DFR / Trade Investment for contract condition or pay-for-performance questions.

subItems.text: Budget, intensity, or frequency guardrails are defined.
Promotion management rules connect to other commercial planning routines.

questions.text: Promotion KPI and ROI measurement framework

anchors.1: Promotion measurement is still basic and does not yet provide a clear view of value creation.
anchors.2: Some promotion KPIs are tracked, but ROI, baseline, and impact logic are not yet fully defined.
anchors.3: Basic promotion KPI and ROI logic are in place and support regular evaluation in the main business context.
anchors.4: Promotion KPIs, baseline logic, and ROI measurement are clearly defined and consistently used.
anchors.5: Promotion measurement is highly robust, decision-oriented, and supports sophisticated optimization over time.

subItems.text: Promotion KPIs have been defined.
ROI, baseline, uplift, or efficiency measurement logic has been defined.
Promotion measurement is supported by regular reporting or tools.

questions.text: Promotion execution tracking and issue resolution

anchors.1: Promotion execution is tracked selectively, and issue resolution is still mostly reactive.
anchors.2: Some promotion execution issues are identified, but follow-up is uneven and not yet fully closed-loop.
anchors.3: Promotion execution is tracked in the main business context, with basic corrective action in place.
anchors.4: Promotion execution is monitored systematically, and issues are resolved through clear routines and accountability.
anchors.5: Promotion execution management is highly disciplined, with rapid issue resolution and strong in-market control.

subItems.text: Promotion execution and spend use are tracked.
Promotion execution issues or deviations can be identified.
Corrective action is taken when promotion execution issues arise.

questions.text: Promotion performance analysis and learning loop

anchors.1: Promotion performance is reviewed at a basic level, with limited structured learning.
anchors.2: Some performance analysis is used, but learning remains partial and does not yet consistently improve future decisions.
anchors.3: Promotion results are analyzed regularly, and the main lessons are used in subsequent planning.
anchors.4: Promotion analysis includes strong performance and learning routines that meaningfully improve future actions.
anchors.5: Promotion learning is highly disciplined and continuously strengthens investment quality, decision-making, and business impact.

subItems.text: Promotion performance is analyzed using baseline, uplift, ROI, or portfolio impact.
Promotion results are translated into actionable learning.
Those learnings are fed back into future promotion decisions.

questions.text: Promotion capability building

anchors.1: Promotion knowledge is still concentrated in a limited group and is not yet broadly developed across teams.
anchors.2: Some capability building exists, but understanding of promotion value creation remains uneven.
anchors.3: Basic promotion capability is being built across the main functions involved in planning and execution.
anchors.4: Promotion capability is developed in a structured, cross-functional way and improves commercial decision quality.
anchors.5: Promotion capability building is embedded, broad-based, and continuously strengthens the organization’s commercial effectiveness.

subItems.text: Promotion capability building or coaching is in place.
Sales, commercial, and finance teams share a common understanding of promotion value creation.
Promotion analysis and management do not depend only on a few individuals.

questions.text: Promotion optimization and future readiness

anchors.1: Promotion management is still focused mainly on immediate needs, with limited future-oriented improvement.
anchors.2: Some optimization is taking place, but future readiness and systematic improvement are still evolving.
anchors.3: Promotion strategy and management are reviewed and improved over time in the core business context.
anchors.4: Promotion management is continuously optimized with stronger data, logic, and future-oriented planning.
anchors.5: Promotion management is fully forward-looking, continuously improved, and used proactively to drive long-term value creation.

subItems.text: Promotion strategy is optimized over time rather than repeated unchanged.
Automation, tools, or data usage are improving promotion management.
Promotion management is moving from short-term volume logic toward longer-term value creation.

questions.text: Trade investment opportunity and issue diagnosis

anchors.1: Trade investment discussions are still developing, with limited clarity on where the most important issues and opportunities sit.
anchors.2: Some trade investment issues are recognized, but the view remains partial across channels or customers.
anchors.3: The main trade investment opportunities and leakage points are identified in the core business context.
anchors.4: Trade investment opportunity and risk are diagnosed in a structured way across channel and account dimensions.
anchors.5: A forward-looking trade investment view is actively maintained and used to guide strategy, policy, and resource allocation.

subItems.text: The main trade investment opportunities and value leakage issues have been identified.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Promotion Spend for promotion mechanic, baseline, or uplift questions.

subItems.text: Trade investment issues are differentiated by channel or account where relevant.
A clear trade investment opportunity / risk view is in place rather than isolated observations.

questions.text: Use of trade investment data and insight inputs

anchors.1: Trade investment assessment relies mainly on broad experience, with limited structured data or customer economics input.
anchors.2: Some trade investment data is used, but analysis and customer insight remain limited or inconsistent.
anchors.3: Relevant trade spend, account economics, and customer inputs are used in regular diagnosis for the main business areas.
anchors.4: Trade investment diagnosis is supported by structured data, customer economics, and behavior-related inputs.
anchors.5: A strong trade investment insight base is used proactively to identify efficiency, value creation, and policy opportunities.

subItems.text: Discount, rebate, DFR, or gross-to-net data is used in diagnosis.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Pricing for market pricing or price perception questions.

subItems.text: Customer or account economics are used to support trade investment decisions.
Customer behavior or execution feedback is used in trade investment assessment.

questions.text: Trade investment strategic direction

anchors.1: Trade investment direction is still evolving and is not yet clearly defined by channel or customer type.
anchors.2: An initial direction exists, but it is not yet translated into clear strategic choices across the business.
anchors.3: Trade investment direction is defined for the main business context and supports regular commercial discussion.
anchors.4: Trade investment strategy is clearly differentiated by channel and account and aligned to value creation goals.
anchors.5: A forward-looking trade investment strategy is fully articulated and proactively shapes commercial policy and customer engagement.

subItems.text: A clear trade investment strategy has been defined.
Trade investment direction is differentiated by channel or account.
Trade investment strategy is aligned to value creation or profitability objectives.

questions.text: Translation of strategy into customer and account approach

anchors.1: Trade investment strategy is not yet consistently translated into customer and account-facing approaches.
anchors.2: Some strategic direction is reflected in account discussions, but translation remains uneven and relationship-based.
anchors.3: The main trade investment principles are translated into basic customer and account approaches.
anchors.4: Trade investment strategy is clearly translated into differentiated customer and account engagement models.
anchors.5: Customer and account approaches are strongly aligned to trade investment strategy and consistently support joint value creation.

subItems.text: Trade investment strategy has been translated into account or customer approach.
subItems.boundaryNote: Use Mix for assortment, picture of success, or mix ambition questions.

subItems.text: Trade investment logic is linked to customer value creation or JBP discussion.
Account-facing teams understand the strategic logic behind trade investment choices.

questions.text: Commercial policy, contract structure, and conditionality

anchors.1: Commercial policy and trade term structure are still developing and are not yet consistently defined.
anchors.2: Some contract and conditionality logic exists, but application is still uneven across customers or channels.
anchors.3: Basic commercial policy, contract structure, and conditionality are in place in the main business context.
anchors.4: Trade terms, conditions, and policy logic are clearly defined and consistently applied across key accounts and channels.
anchors.5: Commercial policy and conditionality are highly disciplined, transparent, and actively used to drive desired customer behavior.

subItems.text: Clear trade terms or contract structure is in place.
Conditionality or pay-for-performance logic is defined.
Investment conditions can be interpreted and applied consistently.

questions.text: Gross-to-net framework and monitoring logic

anchors.1: Gross-to-net visibility is still limited and does not yet consistently support management decisions.
anchors.2: Some gross-to-net analysis exists, but tracking and bucket visibility remain partial.
anchors.3: Basic gross-to-net and trade spend visibility is in place for the main business context.
anchors.4: Gross-to-net logic, monitoring, and bucket-level visibility are clear and consistently used in decision-making.
anchors.5: Gross-to-net management is highly robust, transparent, and actively supports strategic trade investment optimization.

subItems.text: A gross-to-net or trade spend analysis framework is in place.
Different trade investment buckets can be tracked.
Monitoring is supported by regular reporting, tools, or routines.

questions.text: Execution and compliance of trade investment rules

anchors.1: Trade investment rules are applied in selected cases, but execution and compliance are not yet consistently managed.
anchors.2: Some compliance issues are identified, but follow-up remains uneven and not always timely.
anchors.3: Trade investment execution and compliance are tracked in the main business context, with basic corrective action.
anchors.4: Trade investment policy execution is systematically monitored, and compliance gaps are managed through clear routines.
anchors.5: Trade investment execution is tightly controlled, with strong compliance management and disciplined corrective action.

subItems.text: Actual execution of trade investment or commercial policy is tracked.
Non-compliance, missed conditions, or policy deviations can be identified.
Corrective action or escalation is used to manage trade investment execution gaps.

questions.text: Measurement of customer behavior and investment effectiveness

anchors.1: Customer response to trade investment is only partially visible, and effectiveness is not yet clearly measured.
anchors.2: Some effectiveness assessment exists, but links to customer behavior and value creation remain limited.
anchors.3: Trade investment effectiveness is reviewed in the main business context, with basic visibility on customer response.
anchors.4: Trade investment effectiveness and customer behavior are measured in a structured way and inform action.
anchors.5: Customer response and investment effectiveness are deeply understood and actively used to optimize future trade investment.

subItems.text: The effect of trade investment on customer behavior is measured.
The effectiveness of trade investment is assessed against business outcomes.
Trade investment review results are translated into future adjustment actions.

questions.text: Trade investment capability and negotiation quality

anchors.1: Trade investment capability is still developing and is concentrated in a limited number of individuals.
anchors.2: Some capability building exists, but negotiation quality and commercial understanding remain uneven.
anchors.3: Basic trade investment and negotiation capability is in place across the main relevant functions.
anchors.4: Trade investment capability is developed in a structured way and improves negotiation discipline and customer management.
anchors.5: Trade investment capability and negotiation quality are embedded strengths across the organization and continuously improved.

subItems.text: Trade investment capability building is in place.
Sales, commercial, and finance teams share a common understanding of trade investment logic.
Trade investment negotiation quality does not rely only on a few experienced individuals.

questions.text: Trade investment improvement and future readiness

anchors.1: Trade investment practices are still mainly driven by current needs, with limited long-term improvement logic.
anchors.2: Some improvement is taking place, but future readiness and ongoing optimization remain uneven.
anchors.3: Trade investment structures and reviews support gradual improvement in the main business context.
anchors.4: Trade investment is systematically reviewed and improved with a clear future-oriented management approach.
anchors.5: Trade investment is managed as a forward-looking value creation lever and continuously optimized over time.

subItems.text: Trade investment structures are optimized over time rather than rolled forward unchanged.
Recurring review is used to improve DFR or trade term structures.
Trade investment is treated as a long-term value creation lever rather than only a cost of doing business.
